case_name: Potable reuse polishing for pesticide- and cyanide-impacted lake water under tight effluent and footprint constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lake water intended for potable reuse where diazinon, cyanide, and fenamiphos must be controlled under strict effluent limits, limited footprint, and moderate cost constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A utility is upgrading a compact advanced water reclamation facility that blends highly treated municipal effluent with lake water stored in an off-stream reservoir used as an environmental buffer. Seasonal agricultural runoff has introduced organophosphorus pesticides, while intermittent upstream industrial drainage has contributed low but significant cyanide, creating a mixed-contaminant polishing challenge before potable reuse. The site has very limited available footprint, so highly compact options are attractive, but the owner cannot accept excessive concentrate disposal difficulty or very high operating burden. The trade-off is between compact high-rejection systems and lower-residual oxidation and adsorption routes that may require tighter process control to reliably meet very low finished-water targets.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve very high reliability for potable reuse, with diazinon and fenamiphos reduced to trace or non-detectable levels and cyanide controlled to a stringent finished-water level with stable compliance.
secondary_goals: avoid difficult brine or concentrate management where possible
provide resilience to seasonal water quality variation

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer minimization of hazardous or high-volume concentrate streams; manageable spent media or sludge is acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate seasonal turbidity and NOM variation while maintaining trace-organic and cyanide compliance

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_high_rate_clari: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter to improve downstream oxidation and adsorption performance
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize diazinon and fenamiphos and convert cyanide toward more biodegradable or controllable forms
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and provide polishing of assimilable organics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticides and oxidation byproducts for robust trace-organic polishing
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier oxidation plus adsorption train is well aligned with strict potable reuse goals for trace pesticides and mixed contaminants
ozonation is effective for many organophosphorus pesticides, while biologically active filtration and GAC improve reliability against partial oxidation products
the train avoids the high-difficulty brine handling associated with membrane concentrate
high-rate clarification and compact biofiltration can be configured to fit tighter footprints than large conventional basins
this option balances strict effluent performance with more manageable residuals and moderate operating complexity
potential_risks: cyanide oxidation performance depends on oxidant dose, pH, and contact time, so process validation is required
bromide in source water creates bromate formation risk during ozonation
GAC replacement frequency may increase if seasonal NOM loading rises
inadequate pretreatment could reduce downstream oxidation and adsorption efficiency

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_compact_DAF: remove algae, suspended solids, and a portion of NOM in a compact pretreatment step
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide front-end adsorption of hydrophobic pesticides and reduce oxidant demand
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: create a low-turbidity barrier and protect advanced oxidation and downstream carbon
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_O3_H2O2: increase destruction of diazinon and fenamiphos and enhance cyanide oxidation potential
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: polish oxidation byproducts and residual organics

applicability_rationale: this route is technically strong for very low trace-organic targets and improves confidence where pesticide destruction must be maximized
DAF plus UF is attractive for compact sites and for variable lake water quality
PAC ahead of oxidation can help dampen influent spikes of hydrophobic pesticides
it ranks below the top option because chemical use, solids handling, and process tuning are more involved
potential_risks: higher process complexity due to PAC dosing, membrane operation, and oxidant optimization
PAC and DAF residuals require routine solids handling
advanced oxidation can increase byproduct management needs if oxidant control is poor
cyanide destruction must still be site-validated rather than assumed from generic AOP performance

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_high_rate_clari: reduce particulate and NOM load before adsorption and oxidation
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: remove diazinon and fenamiphos and provide staged adsorption robustness
core_unit_functions.chlorination_with_controlled_breakpoint_: oxidize free cyanide and provide additional chemical barrier
core_unit_functions.dechlorination: quench excess oxidant prior to final UV and distribution stabilization

applicability_rationale: dual-bed GAC is a pragmatic route for hydrophobic organophosphorus pesticides and is simpler than membrane systems
controlled chlorination can be effective for cyanide management under appropriate pH and contact conditions
this route can be cost-competitive and more straightforward than AOP-heavy alternatives
it ranks below oxidation-plus-biofiltration options because oxidation byproduct control and trace-level assurance for potable reuse are less robust
potential_risks: chlorination may not provide as strong a barrier for all pesticide transformation products as ozone-based systems
formation of chlorinated byproducts must be managed, especially with NOM present
GAC performance can deteriorate more quickly under elevated DOC episodes
cyanide speciation affects oxidation efficiency and compliance margin

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong physical rejection of many dissolved contaminants including pesticides and some cyanide species
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_of_permeate: polish any low-molecular-weight organics passing the membrane and strengthen potable reuse barrier performance
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is highly attractive under space constraints because of compact footprint and strong broad-spectrum rejection
this option is technically capable and can achieve very high finished-water quality for potable reuse
it ranks lower because concentrate management conflicts with the stated residuals preference and because cost and O&M burden are higher than needed for this contaminant set
for cyanide, concentrate-side management becomes a significant liability even if permeate quality is excellent
potential_risks: brine or concentrate containing rejected pesticides and cyanide requires difficult handling and disposal planning
membrane fouling risk is elevated with lake-water NOM and seasonal particulates unless pretreatment is tightly controlled
high-pressure operation increases energy demand and operator skill requirements
additional post-treatment and integrity management are required for stable reuse operation

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and some NOM before resin and UV systems
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange_or_specialty_resin: target dissolved cyanide species and selected polar organics depending on resin selection

applicability_rationale: this option is included as a plausible but lower-ranked route where cyanide-specific removal is prioritized
specialty resin can assist with cyanide control, and UV-based oxidation can address remaining trace organics

it ranks last because performance against mixed pesticides is less straightforward, resin selection is matrix-sensitive, and the overall train is less proven as a balanced potable reuse solution for this exact mixture
footprint can be acceptable, but chemical and media management complexity weakens the fit
potential_risks: resin exhaustion and selectivity can be strongly affected by competing anions and NOM
spent regenerant or spent media may create a hazardous residual management issue
UV_H2O2 alone may be less efficient if upstream organics are not sufficiently reduced
overall compliance confidence for both cyanide and pesticides may be weaker than with the top-ranked multi-barrier options

preferred_rank_pattern: oxidation_plus_biological_or_adsorptive_polishing should rank near the top for mixed pesticide and cyanide control
membrane-based compact options should appear as technically strong alternatives but not rank first because concentrate handling conflicts with constraints
single-mechanism or matrix-sensitive options should rank lower than robust multi-barrier trains

must_reflect: strict potable reuse effluent requirements push recommendations toward multi-barrier treatment
space limitation increases attractiveness of compact systems but does not override residuals and cost concerns
moderate cost constraint should penalize unnecessarily expensive high-pressure membrane routes
residuals and concentrate handling must influence ranking rather than only contaminant removal capability

must_contain: why organophosphorus pesticides favor oxidation and activated carbon polishing approaches
why cyanide control requires careful oxidation or targeted removal validation rather than assuming generic trace-organic treatment is sufficient
why membrane options are downgraded despite compact footprint and strong contaminant rejection
why bromide and NOM matter for oxidation-based process selection and byproduct risk

should_mention: bromate or disinfection byproduct formation risk
seasonal NOM and turbidity impacts on adsorption or oxidation demand
spent carbon, sludge, or concentrate management
